va-rural-telehealth-expansion.title: Bring the telehealth that works to rural clinics
va-rural-telehealth-expansion.description: The Department of Veterans Affairs must fund the expansion of its proven telehealth program to every rural clinic in the country.
va-rural-telehealth-expansion.messageBody:
It is frustrating to know that a solution to the rural healthcare crisis already exists, yet it remains out of reach for so many who served. We know that VA telehealth works—it has already served millions and drastically cut wait times—but for a veteran in a remote town, a proven program doesn't matter if it isn't available at their local clinic.

[Personal Connection]

Recent developments show that the technology is ready. In rural Texas, 'hands-on' telehealth is already allowing technicians to use stethoscopes and otoscopes so physicians can hear heart and lung sounds from miles away, removing the burden from veterans who are often older or less tech-savvy [3]. This isn't a pilot project; it is a lifeline that needs to be standard equipment.

The pressure to close this gap is growing. Just two days ago, the Senate Veterans’ Affairs Committee passed bipartisan legislation that would require the VA to establish these exact types of agreements between VA medical centers and civilian facilities in rural areas [2]. Over 80 members of Congress have already signaled to the Secretary that they expect the Office of Rural Health to prioritize these creative public-private partnerships [4].

You have the authority to move this forward now. I am asking you to fully implement the proposed telehealth grant program to fund access points in non-VA facilities and eliminate copayments for these vital services [1]. Please don't wait for a mandate to do what we already know works. Expand these partnerships to every rural clinic so that a veteran's zip code no longer determines the quality of their care.

modernize-coppa-protections.title: Our children's privacy is still stuck in 1998
modernize-coppa-protections.description: Kids spend 7 hours a day online. Companies harvest 72 million data points per child per year. Federal privacy law hasn't updated since 1998 — COPPA is older than the kids it's supposed to protect.
modernize-coppa-protections.messageBody:
It is a disturbing absurdity that the law meant to protect our children online was written in 1998—an era before smartphones, social media, and the constant harvesting of their digital lives. As the FTC recently acknowledged, there has been an 'explosion' in internet-connected technology since COPPA was enacted nearly thirty years ago [1]. Today, COPPA is literally older than the children it is supposed to protect.

[Personal Connection]

We are currently witnessing the consequences of this frozen-in-time policy. Just last month, legal analysis highlighted how 'kidfluencers' are being pushed to produce hundreds of videos, sometimes working twelve hours a day, seven days a week, with zero compensation and no privacy [2]. Because our frameworks haven't kept pace, we are allowing a new generation of digital exploitation to flourish under the guise of 'content creation.'

This isn't just about screen time; it is about the mass collection of 'personally identifiable sensitive data,' including geolocation and biometric information, by entities that view our children as data mines rather than people [3]. While the FTC has begun looking into age-verification technologies to bridge this gap [4], the pace of regulation is still losing to the pace of extraction.

I am asking you to move beyond 'reminders' and policy statements. We need aggressive enforcement of existing labor and privacy codes against those exploiting minors for profit, and a modernized federal standard that recognizes the digital realities of 2026. Our children should not be forced to rely on 20th-century protections for 21st-century violations.

oregon-treatment-not-prison.title: Choose treatment that works over the high cost of prison
oregon-treatment-not-prison.description: The State of Oregon must prioritize investment in drug treatment courts over incarceration to save taxpayer money and keep families together.
oregon-treatment-not-prison.messageBody:
It is heartbreaking to watch our state choose the most expensive way to fail families. When we send someone to prison for a drug-related offense, we aren’t just spending taxpayer dollars; we are paying a premium to ensure a family is broken and a cycle of recidivism continues, even when a cheaper, more effective path is right in front of us.

[Personal Connection]

The fiscal reality is staggering. In Oregon, it costs approximately $184 per day—$67,273 a year—to incarcerate one person [1]. By contrast, the Family Sentencing Alternative Program (FSAP) operates at just $27 per day, or $9,855 annually [1]. We are choosing to spend nearly seven times more money for a process that often yields worse outcomes. Even new digital recovery ecosystems supported by courts can deliver results for as little as $1,800 per year, compared to the $36,500 to $73,000 we waste on a single year of jail time [2].

Even those who prioritize traditional law enforcement and 'stopping crime' acknowledge that the 'poison of fentanyl and addiction' must be confronted directly [4]. The federal government is already moving to provide resources to combat this crisis [3], but Oregon must decide how to use its own power. We cannot afford—morally or financially—to keep choosing incarceration over the treatment courts that actually keep our neighbors stable and our families together.

I am asking you to prioritize and expand funding for drug treatment courts and alternative sentencing programs like FSAP in the current budget. Please choose the investment that saves both money and lives.

portland-affordable-home-bans.title: Stop blocking the homes we can actually afford
portland-affordable-home-bans.description: The City of Portland must remove the regulatory barriers to 3D-printed housing and community land trusts to provide homes at a fraction of traditional construction costs.
portland-affordable-home-bans.messageBody:
It is exhausting to watch the cost of a standard home climb toward half a million dollars while knowing that 3D-printed homes can be built for a fraction of that cost. We are living through a housing crisis where the technology to solve it exists, but the rules to allow it don't. It feels like we are choosing bureaucratic inertia over actual roofs. 

[Personal Connection]

Last month, the City Council took a vital step by voting unanimously to rezone properties specifically to support the community land trust model [1]. As the city noted, these zoning map changes are essential to 'avoid going through a lengthy and uncertain and expensive land use process and get to construction faster' [1]. But rezoning is only half the battle if the building codes themselves remain a barrier to innovation.

The city's ongoing Affordable Housing Opportunities Project aims to support non-profit partners by 'reducing zoning designation barriers' [2], and the Housing Bonus Alignment Project is currently proposing code changes to increase production [4]. However, we need to move faster. Even at the federal level, recent guidance directs officials to eliminate 'arbitrary or unreasonable building code standards' that hinder alternative construction methods [3].

Mayor Wilson, Director Kutch, and Ms. Didion: I am asking you to proactively clear the path for 3D-printed housing and expand the land trust model. Please ensure that our permitting and development standards reflect the urgency of this crisis. We have the tools to make Portland affordable; we just need you to stop the regulations from blocking them.

parks-canada-maintenance-neglect.title: Our parks pay for themselves yet continue to decay
parks-canada-maintenance-neglect.description: The federal government must reinvest the massive revenue generated by visitors into fixing the crumbling infrastructure across Parks Canada sites.
parks-canada-maintenance-neglect.messageBody:
It is difficult to reconcile the fact that Canada’s national parks generate $3.3 billion in visitor spending on a $900 million budget—a 3.6x return—while the government allows a $3.6 billion maintenance backlog to persist. We are witnessing a fiscal absurdity: a premier national asset that literally pays for itself is being left to crumble. 

[Personal Connection]

When I see the state of our facilities, it doesn’t just feel like a lack of funding; it feels like a lack of vision. While Canada manages a staggering backlog of repairs, other jurisdictions are treating nature as the critical infrastructure it is. For example, the Wildlife Conservation Board in California recently approved nearly $60 million in grants specifically to protect biodiversity and improve public access [2]. They are also utilizing dedicated funding streams to award over $54 million for coastal resilience and restoration projects [3]. 

Even in the realm of transportation and conservation, our competitors are moving forward while we stall. California is currently completing the world’s largest wildlife crossing with an $18.8 million allocation from a program designed specifically to mitigate the environmental impacts of infrastructure [1]. These are the investments of a country that understands its natural assets are its most valuable economic engine.

I am asking you to address this imbalance by creating a dedicated reinvestment strategy that directs visitor-generated revenue back into the parks themselves. We cannot continue to treat our parks as a revenue source while neglecting the very foundations that make them accessible. Please prioritize the elimination of the maintenance backlog in the next budget cycle to ensure our parks remain viable for both the environment and our economy.

ontario-libraries-debt-free-careers.title: No tuition, no debt, just an Ontario library card
ontario-libraries-debt-free-careers.description: The Government of Ontario must scale the debt-free coding bootcamp model to every public library in the province.
ontario-libraries-debt-free-careers.messageBody:
There is a profound sense of relief in the idea that the path to a stable career doesn't have to begin with a mountain of debt. The most powerful tool for economic mobility in Ontario isn't a private loan or a restricted grant—it is a library card. The fact that our public libraries have already placed 2,400 people into tech jobs without tuition or waitlists proves that the 'gatekeepers' of education are no longer necessary.

[Personal Connection]

Right now, the average cost for a private coding bootcamp in Canada ranges from $12,000 to over $17,000 [2]. For many of us, that price tag is a dead end. We are told that 50% of modern jobs require skills beyond a high school diploma but different from a traditional degree, yet the path to those 'middle skills' is often blocked by these high costs [3]. Our libraries are already bridging this gap. As recently as last month, the Toronto Public Library demonstrated its unique ability to manage complex economic programs, having helped residents access more than $5.3 million in refunds and benefits through its financial empowerment services [1].

We don't need to reinvent the wheel; we need to fuel the one that is already turning. I am asking the Ministry of Education, the Ministry of Economic Development, and the Ministry of Tourism, Culture and Gaming to coordinate with the Federation of Ontario Public Libraries to scale this debt-free coding bootcamp model to every branch in the province. 

Please invest in a system where the only requirement for a better life is a library card.

bc-energy-revenue-parity.title: Our land powers BC while we receive crumbs
bc-energy-revenue-parity.description: The Government of British Columbia must ensure resource revenue sharing matches the scale of clean energy contribution from First Nations communities.
bc-energy-revenue-parity.messageBody:
It is a jarring reality to live in a community that powers the rest of British Columbia while receiving only a tiny fraction of the revenue generated from our own territories. If reconciliation is going to mean anything in this province, the revenue sharing must finally match the contribution. We are providing the energy that runs BC, yet we are left with the crumbs of the economic benefit.

[Personal Connection]

The disparity is becoming impossible to ignore, especially as the federal government moves toward a true nation-to-nation relationship. The recent šxʷq̓ʷal̕təl̕tən agreement with the Musqueam formally recognizes unextinguished rights and title [1], setting a precedent that the provincial government cannot continue to overlook. While Indigenous Services Canada is planning to spend over $23.7 billion on Indigenous well-being and self-determination this year alone [4], BC's provincial resource-sharing models remain stuck in an era of extraction rather than partnership.

With Canada’s clean energy GDP projected to reach $107 billion over the next five years [2], First Nations should be leading this growth, not watching from the sidelines. Research into community energy models shows that localized credit redistribution can reduce energy costs by up to 35% [3], proving that First Nations are high-value partners capable of driving the grid’s efficiency. 

I am asking you to immediately redesign the provincial resource revenue sharing model to reflect the actual scale of clean energy contributions from First Nations. We are looking for a commitment that matches the federal government's shift toward genuine self-determination and recognizes our title as the foundation of BC's energy future [1][4].

montreal-bixi-savings.title: Montreal’s BIXI program saves more in health than it costs
montreal-bixi-savings.description: Montreal's BIXI bike-share program cut downtown car trips by 12% and saves $14 million per year in healthcare costs from reduced pollution. It costs the city $5 per resident per year.
montreal-bixi-savings.messageBody:
When the BIXI stations return to our streets each spring, it is more than just a change in season—it is the moment Montreal feels like itself again. This program is a source of pride because it represents the first time North America saw what a modern, active city could look like, and it started right here with us. We aren't just moving from point A to point B; we are participating in a collective achievement that makes our neighborhoods quieter, our air cleaner, and our residents healthier.

[Personal Connection]

We are currently at a critical juncture for urban mobility. National data shows that transit recovery rates have slipped to 82.2% of 2019 volumes, down from the previous year [4]. In this environment, BIXI is not a luxury; it is a vital necessity. Peer-reviewed research specifically focused on Montreal confirms that access to high-comfort cycling infrastructure increases the odds of residents choosing to bike by 28% [1]. By cutting downtown car trips by 12%, BIXI does the heavy lifting that major infrastructure projects strive for. For comparison, the REM is valued for its ability to remove 30,000 cars from the road daily [3]; BIXI achieves similar goals at the remarkably low cost of just $5 per resident per year.

The math is undeniable. Recent studies on bike-sharing ROI show that every dollar spent generates a 10% annual return in positive externalities, preventing chronic diseases and creating massive healthcare savings [2]. For Montreal, this translates to a $14 million annual dividend in public health. To let this program stagnate is to ignore a primary tool for sustainable development and fiscal responsibility.

I am calling on the Commission and the City of Montreal to secure BIXI’s long-term funding and accelerate the expansion of high-comfort cycling paths. We need to treat BIXI as the essential public health infrastructure it is. Please ensure that this symbol of Montreal’s innovation remains a robust, accessible, and permanent part of our identity.

apple-fifteen-minutes.title: Our year of work is just fifteen minutes of interest
apple-fifteen-minutes.description: Apple must raise retail wages to reflect the company's vast wealth and interest income.
apple-fifteen-minutes.messageBody:
It is a staggering reality that fifteen minutes of Apple’s daily interest income is equivalent to the entire annual salary of a retail worker. For those of us on the front lines of the brand, knowing that the company’s idle cash is working harder than we are creates a sense of profound disconnection.

[Personal Connection]

This isn't just a grievance; it’s a reflection of an economy where 60% of Americans are living paycheck to paycheck while CEOs earn 290 times more than their average worker [3]. When a company holds $162 billion in cash reserves, a starting wage of $22/hour isn't a market rate—it is a choice to keep the people who build the brand in a state of constant financial anxiety.

There is a growing, bipartisan consensus that this level of inequality is unsustainable. Currently, 62% of Americans—including 52% of Republicans—favor capping CEO pay relative to worker pay [1]. The Tax Excessive CEO Pay Act (H.R. 5298) would create a direct financial consequence for this disparity, increasing corporate tax rates by up to 5% for companies with pay ratios exceeding 500 to 1 [2]. As we have seen with similar proposals, the potential tax liability for major corporations could reach $100 billion over the next decade [4].

We are asking for a correction before this gap becomes a permanent rupture:

- For Apple: Substantially increase retail wages to reflect the company’s immense wealth and the value provided by its workforce.
- For the Senate HELP Committee: Support the Tax Excessive CEO Pay Act to ensure that corporate tax structures encourage fair pay.
- For Bloomberg News: Provide rigorous coverage of the financial and social risks inherent in a business model that prioritizes interest income over human labor.

We are watching how these resources are allocated, and we will remember who chose to value people as much as they value their reserves.

preschool-success-model.title: Preschool for every child pays for itself and our future
preschool-success-model.description: Our state must adopt the model of Colorado's universal preschool program to deliver immediate savings for families and lasting economic benefits.
preschool-success-model.messageBody:
It is rare to find a policy where the math is so overwhelmingly on the side of the people, yet the struggle to access it remains so difficult. We know that every dollar invested in universal preschool returns $4.30 in reduced remediation and increased lifetime earnings. This isn't just a budget line item; it is a fundamental economic engine that pays for itself by allowing parents to work while ensuring our children don't start their lives behind an invisible finish line.

[Personal Connection]

Across the country, we are seeing the recognition that childcare is essential infrastructure. Just this week, Adams County launched a program providing up to 100% property tax relief for childcare providers, specifically to keep families 'healthy, strong, and thriving' [2]. This follows the clear mandate from voters in Larimer County who approved a sales tax specifically to stabilize the early childhood workforce and expand provider capacity [1].

We cannot afford to ignore the success of these models. In Pennsylvania, the administration is scaling recruitment and retention bonuses to a $35 million total investment because they recognize that access is impossible without a supported workforce [3]. Even as we look at benchmarks for subsidized care, like the 85% of state median income threshold used to determine eligibility [4], it is clear that the middle class is being squeezed out of the opportunity to give their children a head start.

I am asking you to champion a universal preschool model that mirrors the success we are seeing in Colorado. We need a system that offers immediate savings to families and long-term economic stability for our state. Please prioritize the expansion of these slots and the direct support of the early childhood workforce. We are watching how you choose to invest in our future, and we will remember who stood up for our families.

heal-the-highway-scar.title: Tear down the highway scar and heal our city
heal-the-highway-scar.description: The City Councils of Dallas, Rochester, and Syracuse must commit to removing urban freeways to restore the health and connection of our neighborhoods.
heal-the-highway-scar.messageBody:
A highway is not just a road; it is a wound that never healed. For decades, these concrete barriers have functioned as scars across Dallas, Rochester, and Syracuse, severing neighborhoods and cutting off the lifeblood of our local economies. We have lived with the noise, the pollution, and the division for long enough. It is time to treat these freeways as the mistakes they are and begin the work of healing our cities.

[Personal Connection]

We already have the blueprint for what happens when we prioritize people over pavement. In Rochester, replacing the below-grade Inner Loop with a street-level boulevard catalyzed the 'Neighborhood of Play,' anchored by a $75 million museum expansion and new residential developments [1]. This transformation proves that removing a scar creates room for a community to breathe and grow. Furthermore, the U.S. Department of Transportation has set a clear goal to reduce roadway fatalities to 36,458 or fewer by 2026 [3]; eliminating dangerous, aging urban freeways is a direct path to achieving that safety milestone.

The resources to act are available now. Congress has approved $64.3 billion for the Federal Highway Administration for FY 2026, including $350 million specifically for bridge repair and rehabilitation [2]. Even the House Appropriations Committee’s call for fiscal discipline and an 'America First' agenda acknowledges the urgent need to reprioritize transportation funding to meet the nation's actual infrastructure needs [4]. 

I am asking you to commit to the removal of urban freeways in our cities. Use the authorized federal funding to replace these scars with human-scale infrastructure that restores our health, our property values, and our connection to one another. We are watching how these billions are spent, and we will remember who chose to keep us divided and who chose to lead us toward recovery.

The City Councils of Dallas, Rochester, and Syracuse must commit to removing urban freeways to restore the health and co

decisionMakers.name: Ryan McCormack
decisionMakers.role: Under Secretary of Transportation for Policy

decisionMakers.name: Maddy Madrazo
decisionMakers.organization: Office of the Mayor, City of Dallas

decisionMakers.name: Marie Therese Dominguez
decisionMakers.organization: New York State Department of Transportation

decisionMakers.name: Rita Paniagua
decisionMakers.role: President of the Syracuse Common Council

decisionMakers.name: Dana K. Miller
decisionMakers.role: Commissioner of Neighborhood and Business Development

decisionMakers.name: Sean Duffy
decisionMakers.title: Secretary of Transportation
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the head of the DOT, he oversees the 'Reconnecting Communities' pilot program and controls the discretionary grant funding necessary for large-scale urban freeway removal. His office sets the national agenda for infrastructure projects that prioritize neighborhood reconnection and urban health.

decisionMakers.name: Sean McMaster
decisionMakers.title: Administrator
decisionMakers.reasoning: He leads the agency responsible for the regulatory approval of changes to the Interstate Highway System and manages the environmental review processes required to decommission urban highways. His authority is critical for the technical and legal transition of highway land back to city use.

decisionMakers.name: Eric L. Johnson
decisionMakers.title: Mayor
decisionMakers.reasoning: He serves as the primary executive champion for Dallas infrastructure and is the lead negotiator with state authorities regarding the future of the I-345 corridor. His support is essential for local legislative commitment to removing urban freeway scars.

decisionMakers.name: Ryan McCormack
decisionMakers.title: Under Secretary of Transportation for Policy
decisionMakers.reasoning: He directs the office responsible for the development of transportation policy, which includes the criteria and strategic goals for urban planning and neighborhood restoration projects. His office influences how federal policy treats highway removal as a public health and connectivity issue.

decisionMakers.name: Maddy Madrazo
decisionMakers.title: Chief of Policy & Communications
decisionMakers.organization: Office of the Mayor, City of Dallas
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the Chief of Policy for the Mayor's office, she acts as a primary gatekeeper and advisor on infrastructure and urban planning initiatives. She is the direct point of contact for policy inquiries and media regarding the Mayor's stance on highway removal.

decisionMakers.name: Steven G. Bradbury
decisionMakers.title: Deputy Secretary of Transportation
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the second-highest official at the DOT, he assists in managing the department's budget and operational priorities, including the allocation of infrastructure grants for urban renewal. He provides institutional leverage over the implementation of large-scale transportation shifts.

decisionMakers.name: Charles Peterson Meachum
decisionMakers.title: Chief of Staff
decisionMakers.reasoning: He manages the Secretary's daily agenda and coordinates high-level decision-making across the department's various administrations. He is a critical gatekeeper for projects seeking the Secretary's direct attention or approval.

decisionMakers.name: Sharon F. Owens
decisionMakers.title: Mayor
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the lead local advocate for the I-81 viaduct removal, she coordinates neighborhood land-use and community health initiatives. Her administration is responsible for the city's housing strategy and regional manufacturing partnerships linked to urban renewal.

decisionMakers.name: Malik Evans
decisionMakers.title: Mayor
decisionMakers.reasoning: He directs city planning resources toward the Inner Loop North transformation project to complete the city's highway removal circuit. He oversees the Office of Financial Empowerment and transformative capital programs aimed at neighborhood reinvestment.

decisionMakers.name: Marie Therese Dominguez
decisionMakers.title: Commissioner
decisionMakers.organization: New York State Department of Transportation
decisionMakers.reasoning: She holds ultimate operational control over the I-81 and Inner Loop assets, making her approval mandatory for any physical removal. She is currently leading the state's Active Transportation Strategic Plan to improve walking and cycling infrastructure.

decisionMakers.name: Rita Paniagua
decisionMakers.title: President of the Syracuse Common Council
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the legislative leader of the council, she has the authority to approve city-level commitments and funding for highway removal projects. She oversees the council's role in delivering city services and infrastructure policy.

decisionMakers.name: Miguel Melendez
decisionMakers.title: City Council President
decisionMakers.reasoning: He leads the legislative body that must approve the budget and zoning changes necessary for the Inner Loop North transformation. His office is a primary gatekeeper for city-wide infrastructure legislation.

decisionMakers.name: Dana K. Miller
decisionMakers.title: Commissioner of Neighborhood and Business Development
decisionMakers.reasoning: He oversees the department responsible for zoning, permitting, and planning the transformation of reclaimed highway land. His role is critical for ensuring highway removal leads to equitable neighborhood revitalization.

decisionMakers.name: Kabutey Ocansey
decisionMakers.title: Commissioner of Environmental Services
decisionMakers.reasoning: He manages the city's engineering, street design, and utility permits, which are essential for the physical removal of highway infrastructure. His department handles the technical execution of street-level connectivity projects.

decisionMakers.name: Alexander Marion
decisionMakers.title: City Auditor
decisionMakers.reasoning: He provides oversight on the value and resource allocation of major city infrastructure projects like the I-81 removal. His independent voice ensures fiscal accountability for large-scale urban planning investments.

decisionMakers.name: Chol Majok
decisionMakers.title: Common Councilor At-Large
decisionMakers.reasoning: As an at-large councilor, he votes on city-wide infrastructure commitments and the legislative framework for neighborhood reconnection. He represents the entire city's interest in the I-81 viaduct project.

decisionMakers.name: Rasheada Caldwell
decisionMakers.title: Common Councilor At-Large
decisionMakers.reasoning: She participates in the legislative approval process for urban planning and community development projects. Her vote is necessary for authorizing the city's participation in highway removal initiatives.

decisionMakers.name: Travis Campbell
decisionMakers.title: District Engineer
decisionMakers.reasoning: Manages the regional highway portfolio and serves as the primary technical gatekeeper for any proposed decommissioning of state-owned highway segments in Dallas. His technical approval is required for any structural changes to these urban corridors.

decisionMakers.name: Chadwick Dabbs
decisionMakers.title: Fort Worth District Contact
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the contact for the Fort Worth District, he manages transportation infrastructure in the western half of the DFW metroplex. His coordination is essential for regional traffic flow and any large-scale highway removals that impact the broader North Texas network.

decisionMakers.name: Michelle Meaux
decisionMakers.title: Austin District Contact
decisionMakers.reasoning: She oversees the Austin District, which is currently implementing major urban highway projects like the I-35 transformation. Her experience with urban freeway capping and removal provides institutional precedent for similar initiatives in Dallas.

decisionMakers.name: Catherine McCreight
decisionMakers.title: Houston District Contact
decisionMakers.reasoning: She serves as the contact for the Houston District, managing one of the most complex urban highway systems in the country. Her office's approach to urban mobility and highway expansion vs. removal sets a significant policy benchmark for other Texas cities.

us-green-card-century-wait.title: Skilled workers shouldn't wait a century for green cards
us-green-card-century-wait.description: The United States government must eliminate the century-long US employment green card backlog.
us-green-card-century-wait.messageBody:
A 134-year wait is not a queue; it is a life sentence in a backlog. We are asking the world’s most talented professionals to move here, build businesses, and raise families, all while knowing the current statutory country caps mean many will likely die before their application is even reviewed [1]. 

[Personal Connection]

This level of dysfunction is a policy choice, not a necessity. Your own agency data confirms the system is failing to keep pace with reality, as USCIS reached the H-2B statutory cap for the entire second half of fiscal year 2026 by March 10th—just ten days into the period [3]. When we project wait times that span over a century for Indian H-1B holders due to the 7% per-country limit, we are effectively telling the people our economy needs most that they will never truly belong here [1].

There is a clear path forward that has already earned the support of the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and bipartisan leadership. The Dignity Act of 2025 is designed to modernize these failing pathways and address the workforce shortages that these backlogs exacerbate [2].

I am asking you to use your administrative and oversight authority to champion the elimination of the per-country caps and support the immediate reforms proposed in the Dignity Act. We need an immigration system that functions on a human timeline, not a centennial one.

[Name]

researchLog: US immigration labor 2026
US Skilled workers shouldn't wait a century for green cards
The United States government must eliminate the century-long US employment green card backlog.

decisionMakers.name: Eric Ruark
decisionMakers.role: Assistant Secretary for Border and Immigration

decisionMakers.name: Joseph B. Edlow
decisionMakers.title: Director
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the Director of USCIS, he oversees the administrative processing of all employment-based green card applications and has the authority to implement policy changes that reduce technical backlogs. His leadership determines how agency resources are allocated to address the multi-decade wait times faced by skilled workers.

decisionMakers.name: Mora Namdar
decisionMakers.title: Assistant Secretary for Consular Affairs
decisionMakers.reasoning: This role is responsible for the Bureau of Consular Affairs, which manages the issuance of visas at embassies and publishes the monthly Visa Bulletin. This bulletin is the primary mechanism that controls the movement of priority dates for the green card backlog.

decisionMakers.name: Brian Gottstein
decisionMakers.title: Associate Director, External Affairs Directorate
decisionMakers.reasoning: He leads the directorate responsible for all external communications and legislative affairs, making him a key gatekeeper for policy advocacy regarding green card backlogs. His office manages the relationship between USCIS and Congress, influencing how legislative proposals are interpreted and implemented by the agency.

decisionMakers.name: Markwayne Mullin
decisionMakers.title: Secretary of Homeland Security
decisionMakers.reasoning: He oversees the entire immigration apparatus and has the authority to issue executive guidance or regulations that can provide relief to those in the backlog. His department manages the agencies responsible for adjudicating green card applications and setting processing priorities.

decisionMakers.name: Eric Ruark
decisionMakers.title: Assistant Secretary for Border and Immigration
decisionMakers.reasoning: Within the Office of Strategy, Policy, and Plans, he is responsible for the policy framework governing immigration. He is a key decision-maker in developing the regulatory solutions required to manage visa caps and backlog relief.

decisionMakers.name: Regan Williams
decisionMakers.title: Director, Oversight and Judiciary
decisionMakers.reasoning: As the primary liaison for the Judiciary committees within the Office of Legislative Affairs, they manage the department's interaction with the legislative bodies that control green card reform. This role is a critical gatekeeper for coordinating policy changes between the executive and legislative branches.
